title: Per capita plastic waste vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: Per capita plastic waste generation rate is measured in kilograms per person per day. GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption:
Per capita plastic waste vs. GDP per capita, 2010

Per capita plastic waste generation rate is measured in kilograms per person per day. GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

This scatter plot maps the relationship between national wealth and plastic waste generation for 2010, plotting per capita plastic waste in kilograms per person per day against GDP per capita in international dollars at 2021 prices on a logarithmic axis. The data points are color-coded by region, distinguishing North America, South America, Africa, Europe, Asia, and Oceania, while circle size corresponds to population. The distribution shows a wide range of waste generation, from near zero in countries like the Democratic Republic of Congo and Tanzania to over 0.6 kilograms per person per day in Antigua and Barbuda and Kuwait. High-income nations generally cluster in the upper-right quadrant, with the United States, Germany, and the Netherlands all generating between 0.3 and 0.5 kilograms daily. However, the pattern is not strictly linear; several lower-income countries appear as outliers with high waste rates relative to their GDP, including Guyana, Sri Lanka, and the Maldives. Conversely, populous Asian nations like India and Indonesia sit in the lower-left, combining low GDP with low per capita waste. European countries such as Sweden, Finland, and Romania show lower waste generation than their high GDP levels might predict, while small island nations like Saint Lucia and Barbados register high waste figures. The chart visualizes a positive association where higher economic output often coincides with higher plastic waste, yet significant deviations exist across different regions and population sizes.
